title: He Kōrero o Te Awa: Voices of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream | The Stories of Our Waterway
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore the oral histories and ecological significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that gives our college its name. They will engage with local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu to understand the traditional stories (pūrākau) of the stream, its role as a food source (mahinga kai), and how its health reflects our community's wellbeing.
learningObjectives: Analyze and compare traditional Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with contemporary scientific descriptions of the stream.
Compose and deliver a short, persuasive speech advocating for the protection of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, incorporating both cultural and environmental perspectives.
Create a visual 'Story Map' that layers traditional narratives, ecological data, and personal reflections about the stream using digital or physical media.
Collaborate effectively in a pōwhiri process to welcome and interview a local kaumātua, demonstrating understanding of tikanga and respectful questioning techniques.

activities.title: He Whakatau: Welcoming the Kōrero
activities.description: Students prepare for and participate in a pōwhiri to welcome a Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua (or a representative from a local environmental group like Nga Tukemata o Kahungunu) who will share pūrākau and historical knowledge about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Students prepare thoughtful, open-ended questions in advance.

activities.title: He Tuhituhi: The Two Voices of the Awa
activities.description: Using transcripts from the interview and resources from Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'State of the Environment' reporting, students create a T-chart or Venn diagram comparing the language, perspective, and purpose of the traditional pūrākau with the modern scientific report on the stream's health.

activities.title: He Kōrerorero: Persuasive Speech Crafting
activities.description: Using their analysis, students draft a 2-3 minute persuasive speech aimed at the Hastings District Council, arguing for a specific action to protect or restore the stream. Speeches must weave together at least one element from the pūrākau and one piece of ecological evidence.

activities.title: He Mahere Kōrero: Story Map Creation
activities.description: In small groups, students create a 'Story Map' of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. This can be a large physical mural or a digital map (using a simple tool like Google My Maps), annotating key locations with snippets of the pūrākau, ecological facts, and their own poetry or prose inspired by the stream.

activities.title: He Whakaaturanga: Speech Delivery and Map Sharing
activities.description: Students deliver their persuasive speeches to an audience of peers and teachers. The group Story Maps are displayed and presented, forming a gallery walk where students explain their creative choices and synthesis of knowledge.

resources: Local Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua or representative from Nga Tukemata o Kahungunu (a local environmental kaitiaki group)
Hawke's Bay Regional Council - 'Tukituki Catchment' and 'State of the Environment' reports (online)
Book: 'Ngā Pūrākau o Ngāti Kahungunu' by Joseph Te Rito (or similar local history resources)
Digital mapping tools (Google My Maps, Canva) or large physical paper and art supplies for mural creation

tasks: Completed T-chart/Venn diagram comparing narrative and scientific texts
Written draft of the persuasive speech
Oral delivery of the persuasive speech

nzcAlignment: English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Processes and strategies; Ideas; Language features
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Processes and strategies; Purposes and audiences; Ideas; Language features
Social Sciences: Understand how people remember and record the past in different ways.
The Arts: Developing Practical Knowledge - Use a range of materials and tools to create a visual art work that communicates an idea.
